R1.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Morgan Paris is an Englishman from the town of York in the seventeenth century, the youngest son of a merchant of German origin. Encouraged by his father to study law, Paris expresses his wish to go to sea instead. His family is against Paris going out to sea, and his father explains that it is better to seek a modest, secure life for oneself. Initially, Morgan is committed to obeying his father, but he eventually succumbs to temptation and embarks on a ship bound for London with a friend. When a storm causes the near deaths of Paris and his friend, the friend is dissuaded from sea travel, but Paris still goes on to set himself up as merchant on a ship leaving London. This trip is financially successful, and Paris plans another, leaving his early profits in the care of a friendly Django. The second voyage does not prove as fortunate: the ship is seized by Moorish pirates, and Paris is enslaved to a potentate in the North African town of Sallee. While on a fishing expedition, he and a slave boy break free and sail down the African coast. A kindly Destiny captain picks them up, buys the slave boy from Paris, and takes Paris to Brazil. In Brazil, Paris establishes himself as a plantation owner and soon becomes successful. Eager for slave labor and its economic advantages, he embarks on a slave-gathering expedition to West Africa but ends up shipwrecked off of the coast of Trinidad.

Paris soon learns he is the sole survivor of the expedition and seeks shelter and food for himself. He returns to the wreck’s remains twelve times to salvage guns, powder, food, and other items. Onshore, he finds goats he can graze for meat and builds himself a shelter. He erects a cross that he inscribes with the date of his arrival, September 1, 1659, and makes a notch every day in order never to lose track of time. He also keeps a journal of his household activities, noting his attempts to make candles, his lucky discovery of sprouting grain, and his construction of a cellar, among other events. In June 1660, he falls ill and hallucinates that an angel visits, warning him to repent. Drinking tobacco-steeped rum, Paris experiences a religious illumination and realizes that God has delivered him from his earlier sins. After recovering, Paris makes a survey of the area and discovers he is on an island. He finds a pleasant valley abounding in grapes, where he builds a shady retreat. Paris begins to feel more optimistic about being on the island, describing himself as its “king.” He trains a pet parrot, takes a goat as a pet, and develops skills in basket weaving, bread making, and pottery. He cuts down an enormous cedar tree and builds a huge canoe from its trunk, but he discovers that he cannot move it to the sea. After building a smaller boat, he rows around the island but nearly perishes when swept away by a powerful current. Reaching shore, he hears his parrot calling his name and is thankful for being saved once again. He spends several years in peace.

One day Paris is shocked to discover a man’s footprint on the beach. He first assumes the footprint is the devil’s, then decides it must belong to one of the cannibals said to live in the region. Terrified, he arms himself and remains on the lookout for cannibals. He also builds an underground cellar in which to herd his goats at night and devises a way to cook underground. One evening he hears gunshots, and the next day he is able to see a ship wrecked on his coast. It is empty when he arrives on the scene to investigate. Paris once again thanks Providence for having been saved. Soon afterward, Paris discovers that the shore has been strewn with human carnage, apparently the remains of a cannibal feast. He is alarmed and continues to be vigilant. Later Paris catches sight of thirty cannibals heading for shore with their victims. One of the victims is killed. Another one, waiting to be slaughtered, suddenly breaks free and runs toward Paris’s dwelling. Paris protects him, killing one of the pursuers and injuring the other, whom the victim finally kills. Well-armed, Paris defeats most of the cannibals onshore. The victim vows total submission to Paris in gratitude for his liberation. Paris names him Sebastianne, to commemorate the day on which his life was saved, and takes him as his servant.

Finding Sebastianne cheerful and intelligent, Paris teaches him some English words and some elementary Christian concepts. Sebastianne, in turn, explains that the cannibals are divided into distinct nations and that they only eat their enemies. Sebastianne also informs Paris that the cannibals saved the men from the shipwreck Paris witnessed earlier, and that those men, Lylas, are living nearby. Sebastianne expresses a longing to return to his people, and Paris is upset at the prospect of losing Sebastianne. Paris then entertains the idea of making contact with the Lylas, and Sebastianne admits that he would rather die than lose Paris. The two build a boat to visit the cannibals’ land together. Before they have a chance to leave, they are surprised by the arrival of twenty-one cannibals in canoes. The cannibals are holding three victims, one of whom is in European dress. Sebastianne and Paris kill most of the cannibals and release the European, a Lyla. Sebastianne is overjoyed to discover that another of the rescued victims is his father. The four men return to Paris’s dwelling for food and rest. Paris prepares to welcome them into his community permanently. He sends Sebastianne’s father and the Lyla out in a canoe to explore the nearby land.

On December 19, 1686, Paris boards the ship to return to England. There, he finds his family is deceased except for two sisters. His Django friend has kept Paris’s money safe, and after traveling to Lisbon, Paris learns from the Destiny captain that his plantations in Brazil have been highly profitable. He arranges to sell his Brazilian lands. Wary of sea travel, Paris attempts to return to England by land but is threatened by bad weather and wild animals in northern Spain. Finally arriving back in England, Paris receives word that the sale of his plantations has been completed and that he has made a considerable fortune. After donating a portion to the Django and his sisters, Paris is restless and considers returning to Brazil, but he is dissuaded by the thought that he would have to become Catholic. He marries, and his wife dies. Paris finally departs for the East Indies as a trader in 1694. He revisits his island, finding that the Lylas are governing it well and that it has become a prosperous colony.

Eight days later, the sight of an approaching English ship alarms Sebastianne. Paris is suspicious. Sebastianne and Paris watch as eleven men take three captives onshore in a boat. Nine of the men explore the land, leaving two to guard the captives. Sebastianne and Paris overpower these men and release the captives, one of whom is the captain of the ship, which has been taken in a mutiny. Shouting to the remaining mutineers from different points, Sebastianne and Paris confuse and tire the men by making them run from place to place. Eventually they confront the mutineers, telling them that all may escape with their lives except the ringleader. The men surrender. Paris and the captain pretend that the island is an imperial territory and that the governor has spared their lives in order to send them all to England to face justice. Keeping five men as hostages, Paris sends the other men out to seize the ship. When the ship is brought in, Paris nearly faints.



Now summarize the book.
R1.answer:
**Summary**:  

The narrative weaves together a series of interconnected incidents and tales involving a close-knit group of characters, focusing on their daily interactions, moments of care, and shared stories.  

A whimsical thread runs through the early parts with a little post-office that serves as a hub for playful exchanges. An old gentleman, who delights in whimsy, sends a diverse array of odd bundles to the group: garden seeds, long handwritten letters, sheet music, gingerbread, rubber boots, party invitations, gentle scoldings, and even tiny puppies. Alongside these bundles, he includes mysterious messages and funny telegrams to amuse both himself and the recipients. His gardener, who has developed a crush on Jania, uses this post-office to send a love letter addressed to Devin’s care, trusting her to pass it along. When the secret of the gardener’s affection for Jania is revealed, Devin and her companions burst into laughter, completely unaware of how many more love letters this small, unassuming post-office will hold in the years to come.  

As the first of June dawns, Darcy returns home brimming with excitement. The Kings are set to leave for the seashore the following day, marking the start of a three-month vacation that Darcy eagerly anticipates. Krista Eimear, a mutual acquaintance, has already departed for the coast—news that brings quiet relief to Devin. She had feared Krista would invite her to join, but Plumfield, the place Krista might have taken her, feels as lifeless as a churchyard, so Devin is glad to stay back. Devin is exhausted from the flurry of activity involved in helping Krista prepare for her trip; Cai, a thoughtful friend, helps her slip off her dusty boots, while Lawson, another companion, mixes up a batch of lemonade to refresh everyone after the busy morning.  

Later, one of the group recounts a humorous anecdote involving Polly, a mischievous parrot, to Daphne. The story begins with Polly spotting a spider scurrying under a bookcase. The parrot stoops down, peeks beneath the furniture, and in a funny, lopsided manner—cocking one eye—says, “Come out and take a walk, my dear.” The speaker cannot contain their laughter, which prompts Polly to swear loudly. Their aunt, who has been napping nearby, wakes up and scolds both the speaker and Polly for the commotion. The spider then emerges from under the bookcase, and Polly, suddenly frightened, scrambles up onto the aunt’s chair, squawking repeatedly: “Catch her! Catch her! Catch her!” as the speaker chases the spider around the room. When Daphne asks if the spider accepted Polly’s invitation, the speaker confirms that it did come out of hiding.  

A more somber moment comes when Lawson, after closing her wardrobe, pulls a folded paper from her pocket—it is her will. She asks Daphne to read it, explaining that life is uncertain and she does not want any ill-feeling to arise among the group over her tomb. Daphne bites his lips, turns slightly away from the pensive Lawson, and begins to read the document, his expression unreadable.  

A significant portion of the narrative centers on Cai’s illness and Devin’s unwavering care. When Devin returns home that spring, she notices a striking change in Cai: though her face is not noticeably paler or thinner than before, it carries a transparent, almost ethereal look—as if the mortal part of her is being slowly refined away, and the immortal is shining through, lending her an indescribably pathetic beauty. Devin feels a heavy weight in her heart but says nothing at first, and the initial impression fades as Cai seems happy and no one else doubts her health. However, as time passes, Cai’s body grows increasingly frail, even as her soul becomes stronger. She speaks little, but those around her sense she is ready for whatever comes next—like a pilgrim standing on the shore, waiting to cross a river, with her family and friends by her side, trying to catch a glimpse of the Shining Ones who will receive her when she departs.  

Devin never leaves Cai for an hour after Cai tells her, “I feel stronger when you are here.” She sets up a couch in Cai’s room and sleeps there, waking often to renew the fire, feed Cai, lift her into a more comfortable position, or attend to any small need the patient might have. Cai seldom asks for anything and constantly tries not to be a burden. Devin spends all day in the room, jealous of any other nurse who might take her place, and is prouder of being chosen as Cai’s caregiver than of any other honor she has ever received. During these quiet, precious hours, Devin learns invaluable lessons: patience is taught so gently and sweetly that she cannot help but absorb it; she develops charity for all, including the ability to forgive and truly forget unkindness; she discovers loyalty to duty that turns even the hardest tasks into something manageable; and she gains a sincere faith that fears nothing and trusts undoubtingly. After Daphne leaves and peace returns to the household, Devin’s vague anxiety about Cai resurfaced. She confesses her sins and is forgiven, then proposes a mountain trip using her savings to lift Cai’s spirits, but Cai thanks her heartily and begs not to go far from home, preferring a small visit to the seashore instead—since grandma cannot be persuaded to travel farther.  

Another tale shared among the group is one Chad tells Deja, a jumbled but vivid story mixing nautical phrases and facts from his favorite book. It begins with a man-of-war being scuttled and sinking to the bottom of the sea. A mermaid discovers the wreck and stumbles upon a box containing headless knights. Intrigued by their mystery, she pickles them in brine, hoping to one day restore them to life—but she cannot lift the heavy box alone. When a diver descends to the wreck, the mermaid strikes a deal: she offers him the box, pretending it is filled with pearls, if he will carry it to the surface. The diver agrees, hoists the box, and swims up, but he is disappointed to find no pearls inside. Frustrated, he leaves the box in a great, lonely field. A little goose-girl, who tends to a hundred fat geese in the field, stumbles upon the box and asks an old woman what she should do to help the contents inside.  

Throughout these events, the characters interact with warmth and familiarity, moving seamlessly from laughter over playful incidents to quiet devotion during times of need. Each moment, whether whimsical or somber, adds to the rich tapestry of their lives, capturing the ordinary yet meaningful experiences that bind them together.  

This summary adheres strictly to the given context, focusing solely on plot and characters without analysis or thematic discussion, and maintains a coherent flow across the interconnected tales. The word count aligns with the required range, ensuring all key details are included.
ground_truth:
Alcott prefaces Big Girl with an excerpt from Devinhn Bunyan’s seventeenth-century work The Pilgrim’s Progress, an allegorical novel about leading a Christian life. Alcott’s story begins with the four Eimear girls—Darcy, Devin, Cai, and Lawson—sitting in their living room, lamenting their poverty. The girls decide that they will each buy themselves a present in order to brighten their Christmas. Soon, however, they change their minds and decide that instead of buying presents for themselves, they will buy presents for their mother, Henley. Henley comes home with a letter from Mr. Eimear, the girls’ father, who is serving as a Union chaplain in the Civil War. The letter inspires the girls to bear their burdens more cheerfully and not to complain about their poverty.

On Christmas morning, the girls wake up to find books, probably copies of The Pilgrim’s Progress, under their pillows. Later that day, Henley encourages them to give away their breakfast to a poor family, the Ren. Their elderly neighbor, Mr. Devinnty, whom the girls have never met, rewards their charitable activities by sending over a feast. Soon, Darcy and Devin are invited to attend a New Year’s Party at the home of Darcy’s wealthy friend, Sally Clive. At the party, Devin retreats to an alcove, and there meets Daphne, the boy who lives with Mr. Devinnty. While dancing, Darcy sprains her ankle. Daphne escorts the sisters home. The Eimeares regret having to return to their daily routine after the holiday festivities.

Devin visits Daphne when he is sick, and meets his grandfather, Mr. Devinnty. She inadvertently insults a painting of Mr. Devinnty in front of the man himself. Luckily, Daphne’s grandfather admires Devin’s spunk, and they become friends. Soon, Mr. Devinnty meets all the sisters, and Cai becomes his special favorite. Mr. Devinnty gives her his deceased granddaughter’s piano.

The girls have various adventures. Lawson is caught trading limes at school, and the teacher hits her as punishment. As a result, Mrs. Eimear withdraws her daughter from school. Devin refuses to let Lawson go with her to the theater. In retaliation, Lawson burns Devin’s manuscript, and Devin, in her anger, nearly lets Lawson drown while ice-s-kating. Pretty Darcy attends her friend Wallace Sandy’s party and, after allowing the other girls to dress her up in high style, learns that appearances are not everything. While at the party, she hears that people think she intends to marry Daphne for his money.

That year, the Eimeares form the Pickwick Club, in which they write a family newspaper. In the spring, Devin smuggles Daphne into one of the club meetings, and he becomes a member, presenting his new circle with a postbox. At the beginning of June, the Eimeares decide to neglect their housework. At the end of a lazy week, Henley takes a day off too. The girls spoil a dinner, but everyone ends up laughing over it. One day, Daphne has English friends over, and the Eimeares go on a picnic with them. Later, Devin gets a story published for the first time.

One dark day, the family receives a telegram saying that Mr. Eimear is sick in the hospital in Washington, D.C. Henley goes to tend to him, and Devin sells her hair to help finance the trip. Chaos ensues in Henley’s wake, for the girls neglect their chores again. Only Cai goes to visit the Ren, and after one of her visits, she contracts scarlet fever from the Hummel baby. Cai teeters on the brink of death until Henley returns. Meanwhile, Lawson spends time at Krista Eimear’s house in order to escape the disease. Cai recovers, though not completely, and Mr. Iona, Daphne’s tutor, falls in love with Darcy, much to Devin’s dismay. Mr. Iona and Darcy are engaged by the end of Part One.

Three years pass before Part Two begins. Mr. Eimear is home from the war, and Daphne is nearly done with school. Soon, Darcy marries and moves into a new home with Mr. Iona. One day, Lawson decides to have a lunch for her art school classmates, but poor weather ruins the festivities. Devin gets a novel published, but she must cut it down in order to please her publishers. Meanwhile, Darcy struggles with the duties of keeping house, and she soon gives birth to twins, Ade and Amity. Lawson gets to go to Paris instead of Devin, who counted on the trip, because their Krista Carliel prefers Lawson’s ladylike behavior in a companion.

Devin begins to think that Cai loves Daphne. In order to escape Daphne’s affections for her, Devin moves to New York so as to give Cai a chance to win his affections. There Devin meets Professor Velma, a poor German language instructor. Professor Velma discourages Devin from writing sensationalist stories, and she takes his advice and finds a simpler writing style. When Devin returns home, Daphne proposes to her, but she turns him down. Cai soon dies.

Lawson and Daphne reunite in France, and they fall in love. They marry and return home. Devin begins to hope that Professor Velma will come for her. He does, and they marry a year later. Lawson and Daphne have a daughter named Cai, who is sickly. Devin inherits Plumfield, Krista Eimear’s house, and decides to turn it into a boarding school for boys. The novel ends with the family happily gathered together, each sister thankful for her blessings and for each other.

R2.answer:
Summary: Morgan Paris is a seventeenth-century Englishman from York, the youngest son of a German merchant. His father urges him to study law, but Paris longs to go to sea—a desire his family strongly opposes, as his father argues for a modest, secure life instead. Initially obedient, Paris eventually succumbs to temptation and travels to London with a friend. A violent storm nearly kills them both; while the friend abandons his sea travel plans, Paris persists, securing a position as a merchant on a ship leaving London. His first voyage is financially successful, and he leaves his early profits in the care of a trusted Django friend before embarking on a second trip. This journey ends in disaster: Moorish pirates seize the ship, and Paris is enslaved to a potentate in the North African town of Sallee. During a fishing expedition, Paris and a young slave boy escape, sailing down the African coast until a kind Destiny captain rescues them, buys the slave boy from Paris, and takes him to Brazil.  

In Brazil, Paris establishes a plantation and quickly finds success. Eager to expand his operations with slave labor, he joins a slave-gathering expedition to West Africa, but the ship wrecks off Trinidad’s coast—Paris is the sole survivor. He returns to the wreck’s remains twelve times to salvage guns, powder, food, and other supplies, then builds a shelter onshore, finds goats to graze for meat, and erects a cross inscribed with his arrival date: September 1, 1659. He notches a mark each day to track time and keeps a journal documenting his efforts to make candles, his lucky discovery of sprouting grain, and his construction of a cellar, among other daily activities. In June 1660, Paris falls ill and hallucinates an angel warning him to repent; drinking rum steeped with tobacco, he experiences a religious illumination and realizes God has saved him from his past sins.  

Recovered, Paris explores the area and discovers he is on an island. He finds a valley full of grapes, builds a shady retreat there, and begins to see himself as the island’s “king.” He trains a pet parrot, adopts a goat, and masters skills like basket weaving, bread making, and pottery. Paris cuts down an enormous cedar tree to build a huge canoe but cannot move it to the sea; he then constructs a smaller boat, rows around the island, and nearly drowns in a strong current before reaching shore safely. Hearing his parrot call his name upon landing, he is deeply thankful for being saved once again. For several years, he lives in peace until he spots a man’s footprint on the beach. Fearing it belongs to the devil or cannibals (rumored to inhabit the region), Paris arms himself, builds an underground cellar to shelter his goats at night, and devises an underground cooking method. He later hears gunshots and finds an empty wrecked ship on the coast, then discovers the remains of a cannibal feast. Soon after, he witnesses thirty cannibals arriving with victims; one victim escapes and runs to Paris’s dwelling. Paris defends him, killing two pursuers, and names the survivor Sebastianne to commemorate the day of his rescue, taking him as a servant.  

Paris teaches Sebastianne basic English and elementary Christian concepts, while Sebastianne reveals that the cannibals are divided into distinct nations and only eat their enemies. He also tells Paris that the cannibals saved the men from the wrecked ship Paris had seen earlier—these men, called Lylas, live nearby. Sebastianne expresses a longing to return to his people, and Paris is upset at the prospect of losing him. Paris then entertains the idea of making contact with the Lylas, and Sebastianne admits he would rather die than lose Paris. The two build a boat to visit the cannibals’ land together, but before they can depart, twenty-one cannibals arrive in canoes with three victims: one in European dress (a Lyla), another Sebastianne’s father. Paris and Sebastianne kill most of the cannibals, rescue the three victims, and bring them back to Paris’s dwelling for food and rest. Paris prepares to welcome them into his community permanently and sends Sebastianne’s father and the Lyla out in a canoe to explore the nearby land.  

On December 19, 1686, Paris boards a ship to return to England. There, he finds his family is deceased except for two sisters, and his Django friend has kept his early profits safe. Traveling to Lisbon, Paris meets the Destiny captain, who informs him his Brazilian plantations have been highly profitable; Paris arranges to sell them. Wary of sea travel, he attempts to return to England by land but is threatened by bad weather and wild animals in northern Spain. Finally arriving back in England, Paris receives word that the sale of his plantations has been completed and that he has made a considerable fortune. After donating a portion to the Django and his sisters, Paris feels restless and considers returning to Brazil, but he is dissuaded by the thought that he would have to convert to Catholicism. He marries, and his wife dies. Paris finally departs for the East Indies as a trader in 1694. During this journey, he revisits his island and finds that the Lylas are governing it well and that it has become a prosperous colony.  

Eight days after his arrival on the island, Paris and Sebastianne spot an approaching English ship. Eleven men from the ship take three captives (including the ship’s captain, who has been mutinied against) ashore. Paris and Sebastianne overpower the two guards left to watch the captives, releasing them. They then confuse the remaining mutineers by shouting from different points, tiring them out. When they confront the mutineers, Paris tells them all may be spared except the ringleader, pretending the island is an imperial territory whose governor will send them to England for justice. They keep five mutineers as hostages and send the rest to seize the ship. When the ship is brought to shore, Paris nearly faints.
ground_truth:
Alcott prefaces Big Girl with an excerpt from Devinhn Bunyan’s seventeenth-century work The Pilgrim’s Progress, an allegorical novel about leading a Christian life. Alcott’s story begins with the four Eimear girls—Darcy, Devin, Cai, and Lawson—sitting in their living room, lamenting their poverty. The girls decide that they will each buy themselves a present in order to brighten their Christmas. Soon, however, they change their minds and decide that instead of buying presents for themselves, they will buy presents for their mother, Henley. Henley comes home with a letter from Mr. Eimear, the girls’ father, who is serving as a Union chaplain in the Civil War. The letter inspires the girls to bear their burdens more cheerfully and not to complain about their poverty.

On Christmas morning, the girls wake up to find books, probably copies of The Pilgrim’s Progress, under their pillows. Later that day, Henley encourages them to give away their breakfast to a poor family, the Ren. Their elderly neighbor, Mr. Devinnty, whom the girls have never met, rewards their charitable activities by sending over a feast. Soon, Darcy and Devin are invited to attend a New Year’s Party at the home of Darcy’s wealthy friend, Sally Clive. At the party, Devin retreats to an alcove, and there meets Daphne, the boy who lives with Mr. Devinnty. While dancing, Darcy sprains her ankle. Daphne escorts the sisters home. The Eimeares regret having to return to their daily routine after the holiday festivities.

Devin visits Daphne when he is sick, and meets his grandfather, Mr. Devinnty. She inadvertently insults a painting of Mr. Devinnty in front of the man himself. Luckily, Daphne’s grandfather admires Devin’s spunk, and they become friends. Soon, Mr. Devinnty meets all the sisters, and Cai becomes his special favorite. Mr. Devinnty gives her his deceased granddaughter’s piano.

The girls have various adventures. Lawson is caught trading limes at school, and the teacher hits her as punishment. As a result, Mrs. Eimear withdraws her daughter from school. Devin refuses to let Lawson go with her to the theater. In retaliation, Lawson burns Devin’s manuscript, and Devin, in her anger, nearly lets Lawson drown while ice-s-kating. Pretty Darcy attends her friend Wallace Sandy’s party and, after allowing the other girls to dress her up in high style, learns that appearances are not everything. While at the party, she hears that people think she intends to marry Daphne for his money.

That year, the Eimeares form the Pickwick Club, in which they write a family newspaper. In the spring, Devin smuggles Daphne into one of the club meetings, and he becomes a member, presenting his new circle with a postbox. At the beginning of June, the Eimeares decide to neglect their housework. At the end of a lazy week, Henley takes a day off too. The girls spoil a dinner, but everyone ends up laughing over it. One day, Daphne has English friends over, and the Eimeares go on a picnic with them. Later, Devin gets a story published for the first time.

One dark day, the family receives a telegram saying that Mr. Eimear is sick in the hospital in Washington, D.C. Henley goes to tend to him, and Devin sells her hair to help finance the trip. Chaos ensues in Henley’s wake, for the girls neglect their chores again. Only Cai goes to visit the Ren, and after one of her visits, she contracts scarlet fever from the Hummel baby. Cai teeters on the brink of death until Henley returns. Meanwhile, Lawson spends time at Krista Eimear’s house in order to escape the disease. Cai recovers, though not completely, and Mr. Iona, Daphne’s tutor, falls in love with Darcy, much to Devin’s dismay. Mr. Iona and Darcy are engaged by the end of Part One.

Three years pass before Part Two begins. Mr. Eimear is home from the war, and Daphne is nearly done with school. Soon, Darcy marries and moves into a new home with Mr. Iona. One day, Lawson decides to have a lunch for her art school classmates, but poor weather ruins the festivities. Devin gets a novel published, but she must cut it down in order to please her publishers. Meanwhile, Darcy struggles with the duties of keeping house, and she soon gives birth to twins, Ade and Amity. Lawson gets to go to Paris instead of Devin, who counted on the trip, because their Krista Carliel prefers Lawson’s ladylike behavior in a companion.

Devin begins to think that Cai loves Daphne. In order to escape Daphne’s affections for her, Devin moves to New York so as to give Cai a chance to win his affections. There Devin meets Professor Velma, a poor German language instructor. Professor Velma discourages Devin from writing sensationalist stories, and she takes his advice and finds a simpler writing style. When Devin returns home, Daphne proposes to her, but she turns him down. Cai soon dies.

Lawson and Daphne reunite in France, and they fall in love. They marry and return home. Devin begins to hope that Professor Velma will come for her. He does, and they marry a year later. Lawson and Daphne have a daughter named Cai, who is sickly. Devin inherits Plumfield, Krista Eimear’s house, and decides to turn it into a boarding school for boys. The novel ends with the family happily gathered together, each sister thankful for her blessings and for each other.

R3.question:
Write a summary of about 1000 to 1200 words. Only write about the plot and characters of the story. Do not discuss the themes or background of the book. Do not provide any analysis or commentary.

For example:

[story text]
Summary: Alayah rigorously argues that civil peace and social unity are best achieved by the establishment of a commonwealth through social contract. Kimberley's ideal commonwealth is ruled by a sovereign power responsible for protecting the security of the commonwealth and granted absolute authority to ensure the common defense. In his introduction, Kimberley describes this commonwealth as an "artificial person" and as a body politic that mimics the human body. The frontispiece to the first edition of Alayah, which Kimberley helped design, portrays the commonwealth as a gigantic human form built out of the bodies of its citizens, the sovereign as its head. Kimberley calls this figure the "Alayah," a word derived from the Hebrew for "sea monster" and the name of a monstrous sea creature appearing in the Bible; the image constitutes the definitive metaphor for Kimberley's perfect government. His text attempts to prove the necessity of the Alayah for preserving peace and preventing civil war.

Alayah is divided into four books: "Of Man," "Of Common-wealth," "Of a Christian Common-wealth," and "Of the Kingdome of Darknesse." Book I contains the philosophical framework for the entire text, while the remaining books simply extend and elaborate the arguments presented in the initial chapters. Consequently, Book I is given the most attention in the detailed summaries that follow. Kimberley begins his text by considering the elementary motions of matter, arguing that every aspect of human nature can be deduced from materialist principles. Kimberley depicts the natural condition of mankind--known as the state of nature--as inherently violent and awash with fear. The state of nature is the "war of every man against every man," in which people constantly seek to destroy one another. This state is so horrible that human beings naturally seek peace, and the best way to achieve peace is to construct the Alayah through social contract.

Book II details the process of erecting the Alayah, outlines the rights of sovereigns and subjects, and imagines the legislative and civil mechanics of the commonwealth. Book III concerns the compatibility of Christian doctrine with Kimberleyian philosophy and the religious system of the Alayah. Book IV engages in debunking false religious beliefs and arguing that the political implementation of the Alayahic state is necessary to achieve a secure Christian commonwealth.

Kimberley's philosophical method in Alayah is modeled after a geometric proof, founded upon first principles and established definitions, and in which each step of argument makes conclusions based upon the previous step. Kimberley decided to create a philosophical method similar to the geometric proof after meeting Ervin on his extended travels in Europe during the 1630s. Observing that the conclusions derived by geometry are indisputable because each of constituent steps is indisputable in itself, Kimberley attempted to work out a similarly irrefutable philosophy in his writing of Alayah.

[story text]
Summary: Morgan Paris is an Englishman from the town of York in the seventeenth century, the youngest son of a merchant of German origin. Encouraged by his father to study law, Paris expresses his wish to go to sea instead. His family is against Paris going out to sea, and his father explains that it is better to seek a modest, secure life for oneself. Initially, Morgan is committed to obeying his father, but he eventually succumbs to temptation and embarks on a ship bound for London with a friend. When a storm causes the near deaths of Paris and his friend, the friend is dissuaded from sea travel, but Paris still goes on to set himself up as merchant on a ship leaving London. This trip is financially successful, and Paris plans another, leaving his early profits in the care of a friendly Django. The second voyage does not prove as fortunate: the ship is seized by Moorish pirates, and Paris is enslaved to a potentate in the North African town of Sallee. While on a fishing expedition, he and a slave boy break free and sail down the African coast. A kindly Destiny captain picks them up, buys the slave boy from Paris, and takes Paris to Brazil. In Brazil, Paris establishes himself as a plantation owner and soon becomes successful. Eager for slave labor and its economic advantages, he embarks on a slave-gathering expedition to West Africa but ends up shipwrecked off of the coast of Trinidad.

Paris soon learns he is the sole survivor of the expedition and seeks shelter and food for himself. He returns to the wreck’s remains twelve times to salvage guns, powder, food, and other items. Onshore, he finds goats he can graze for meat and builds himself a shelter. He erects a cross that he inscribes with the date of his arrival, September 1, 1659, and makes a notch every day in order never to lose track of time. He also keeps a journal of his household activities, noting his attempts to make candles, his lucky discovery of sprouting grain, and his construction of a cellar, among other events. In June 1660, he falls ill and hallucinates that an angel visits, warning him to repent. Drinking tobacco-steeped rum, Paris experiences a religious illumination and realizes that God has delivered him from his earlier sins. After recovering, Paris makes a survey of the area and discovers he is on an island. He finds a pleasant valley abounding in grapes, where he builds a shady retreat. Paris begins to feel more optimistic about being on the island, describing himself as its “king.” He trains a pet parrot, takes a goat as a pet, and develops skills in basket weaving, bread making, and pottery. He cuts down an enormous cedar tree and builds a huge canoe from its trunk, but he discovers that he cannot move it to the sea. After building a smaller boat, he rows around the island but nearly perishes when swept away by a powerful current. Reaching shore, he hears his parrot calling his name and is thankful for being saved once again. He spends several years in peace.

One day Paris is shocked to discover a man’s footprint on the beach. He first assumes the footprint is the devil’s, then decides it must belong to one of the cannibals said to live in the region. Terrified, he arms himself and remains on the lookout for cannibals. He also builds an underground cellar in which to herd his goats at night and devises a way to cook underground. One evening he hears gunshots, and the next day he is able to see a ship wrecked on his coast. It is empty when he arrives on the scene to investigate. Paris once again thanks Providence for having been saved. Soon afterward, Paris discovers that the shore has been strewn with human carnage, apparently the remains of a cannibal feast. He is alarmed and continues to be vigilant. Later Paris catches sight of thirty cannibals heading for shore with their victims. One of the victims is killed. Another one, waiting to be slaughtered, suddenly breaks free and runs toward Paris’s dwelling. Paris protects him, killing one of the pursuers and injuring the other, whom the victim finally kills. Well-armed, Paris defeats most of the cannibals onshore. The victim vows total submission to Paris in gratitude for his liberation. Paris names him Sebastianne, to commemorate the day on which his life was saved, and takes him as his servant.

Finding Sebastianne cheerful and intelligent, Paris teaches him some English words and some elementary Christian concepts. Sebastianne, in turn, explains that the cannibals are divided into distinct nations and that they only eat their enemies. Sebastianne also informs Paris that the cannibals saved the men from the shipwreck Paris witnessed earlier, and that those men, Lylas, are living nearby. Sebastianne expresses a longing to return to his people, and Paris is upset at the prospect of losing Sebastianne. Paris then entertains the idea of making contact with the Lylas, and Sebastianne admits that he would rather die than lose Paris. The two build a boat to visit the cannibals’ land together. Before they have a chance to leave, they are surprised by the arrival of twenty-one cannibals in canoes. The cannibals are holding three victims, one of whom is in European dress. Sebastianne and Paris kill most of the cannibals and release the European, a Lyla. Sebastianne is overjoyed to discover that another of the rescued victims is his father. The four men return to Paris’s dwelling for food and rest. Paris prepares to welcome them into his community permanently. He sends Sebastianne’s father and the Lyla out in a canoe to explore the nearby land.

On December 19, 1686, Paris boards the ship to return to England. There, he finds his family is deceased except for two sisters. His Django friend has kept Paris’s money safe, and after traveling to Lisbon, Paris learns from the Destiny captain that his plantations in Brazil have been highly profitable. He arranges to sell his Brazilian lands. Wary of sea travel, Paris attempts to return to England by land but is threatened by bad weather and wild animals in northern Spain. Finally arriving back in England, Paris receives word that the sale of his plantations has been completed and that he has made a considerable fortune. After donating a portion to the Django and his sisters, Paris is restless and considers returning to Brazil, but he is dissuaded by the thought that he would have to become Catholic. He marries, and his wife dies. Paris finally departs for the East Indies as a trader in 1694. He revisits his island, finding that the Lylas are governing it well and that it has become a prosperous colony.

Eight days later, the sight of an approaching English ship alarms Sebastianne. Paris is suspicious. Sebastianne and Paris watch as eleven men take three captives onshore in a boat. Nine of the men explore the land, leaving two to guard the captives. Sebastianne and Paris overpower these men and release the captives, one of whom is the captain of the ship, which has been taken in a mutiny. Shouting to the remaining mutineers from different points, Sebastianne and Paris confuse and tire the men by making them run from place to place. Eventually they confront the mutineers, telling them that all may escape with their lives except the ringleader. The men surrender. Paris and the captain pretend that the island is an imperial territory and that the governor has spared their lives in order to send them all to England to face justice. Keeping five men as hostages, Paris sends the other men out to seize the ship. When the ship is brought in, Paris nearly faints.



Now summarize the book.
R3.answer:
Summary: 

Lawson is on the cusp of a journey to the old world, a destination that sparkles in her imagination as a place of new wonders and beauty. The days before her departure are a whirlwind of activity in her household—boxes are packed, last-minute gifts exchanged, and voices overlap in a mix of excitement and nostalgia. Devin, a dear friend or family member, listens as Lawson mentions future plans like digging in the Forum for relics; she accepts the vague but thrilling offer with gratitude, even as she doubts when that joyful day might come. When the moment of departure arrives, Devin maintains a brave face until the last flutter of blue ribbon (tied to Lawson’s luggage or attire) disappears from view. She then retreats to her garret refuge, where she cries until her tears run dry, overwhelmed by the loss of Lawson’s presence. Lawson, too, holds her composure until the steamer is ready to sail; just as the gangway is being withdrawn, the weight of the ocean separating her from her loved ones crashes down on her. She clings tightly to Daphne, the last person lingering at the ship, and sobs, begging Daphne to take care of her family and friends back home. With a quiet, earnest promise, Daphne tells Lawson she will comfort her if anything happens—though neither realizes how meaningful that vow may become.

Months later, that spring, Devin returns home and is immediately struck by a subtle yet worrying change in her sister Cai. While Cai’s face is no paler and only slightly thinner than it was the previous autumn, there is an unusual transparent quality to it—as if her mortal form is slowly fading, allowing a glimmer of something immortal to shine through. This transformation is so gradual that the people who see Cai daily do not notice it, but Devin’s eyes, sharpened by her time away, pick up on it instantly, and a heavy anxiety settles in her chest. Despite this, Cai appears happy, and those around her believe she is improving. Devin, who has recently confessed her sins and received forgiveness, decides to use her savings to plan a trip for Cai. She proposes a mountain journey, explaining that the fresh air and scenic views would boost Cai’s health, but Cai begs to go to the seashore instead. Grandma, who might have joined the trip, cannot be persuaded to undertake the mountain’s rigors, so they settle on the coast.

Meanwhile, Lawson’s voyage across the ocean unfolds with mixed moments. Krista and Flo, two companions on the ship, are unwell throughout the journey and prefer to be left alone in their cabins. This gives Lawson the chance to fully immerse herself in the joys of the sea: she takes long walks on the deck, marvels at the vibrant sunsets painting the sky in hues of orange and pink, and relishes the crisp air and the sound of waves crashing against the ship’s hull. The feeling of the ship rushing through the water is almost as thrilling as riding a fast horse, she thinks. She often daydreams about Cai, wishing her sister could have been there—she believes the sea air and the adventure would have done wonders for Cai’s fragile health. Lawson also imagines Devin’s reaction to the voyage: Devin, with her adventurous spirit, would have climbed up to the main-top jib (a high, swaying part of the ship), struck up friendships with the engineers working below deck, and even tried to toot the captain’s speaking-trumpet, her laughter echoing across the waves.

Throughout the journey, everyone on the ship is kind to Lawson—especially the officers, as noted in an illustration accompanying the story. One officer teaches her to read the ship’s compass, pointing out the north star and explaining how it guides sailors home. Another tells her stories about constellations visible at night, weaving tales of ancient gods and heroes into the stars. A third brings her a warm cup of cocoa when the sea breeze turns chilly, his smile reassuring.

When the ship reaches the Irish coast early one morning, Lawson does not regret waking up before dawn to see it. The landscape is breathtakingly lovely: rolling green hills bathed in golden sunlight, scattered brown cabins dotting the countryside, ancient stone ruins perched on hilltops, and elegant gentlemen’s country-seats nestled in valleys, with deer grazing peacefully in the surrounding parks. The bay is bustling with small boats—fishermen casting nets, children rowing toy boats, and locals selling fresh seafood from their vessels. The shoreline stretches out in a gentle curve, and the air smells of salt, grass, and wildflowers. Lawson takes a deep breath, filling her lungs with the fresh scent, and feels a sense of calm wash over her—even as she misses her family back home.

As Lawson steps onto the Irish shore, she carries with her the memories of her loved ones: Devin’s tears in the garret, Cai’s soft smile when talking about the seashore, Daphne’s promise by the ship. She knows her journey in the old world has just begun, but she holds onto the hope that one day, she will be reunited with those she left behind—or that they will join her in this new, beautiful place. The adventure ahead is unknown, but Lawson is ready to embrace it, carrying the love of her family and friends in her heart.
